title: Overage Psychology

content.statement: Variable pricing must feel controlled, not punitive.
content.question: If your business ever charges per-use or overage, how do you make it feel like the user is in control?
sop: Map every situation where your pricing could produce a variable or surprise charge (overage fees, usage tiers, add-ons).
Rate customer Tolerance 1-10 for that kind of variability given your industry and audience (see calculation, page 6).
Rate Predictability 1-10, can customers estimate the charge before it happens (see calculation, page 6).
Rate Control 1-10, can customers actively manage or cap what they're charged (see calculation, page 6).
Rate Surprise 1-10 for how often the actual charge differs from what customers expected (see calculation, page 6), and add a real-time usage indicator or spending cap wherever Surprise is high.
content.explainer: Variable pricing that surprises = churn. Variable pricing the user predicts = love.
questions: If your business ever charges per-use or overage, how do you make it feel like the user is in control?
Would your customers rather get an unexpected discount or an unexpected extra charge, and does your billing ever risk the second one?
Can a customer predict, before they use more, roughly what it will cost them?

definitions.def: An extra charge for going over an included limit, like fees for exceeding your data or usage plan.

content.equation_example: Rate each 1-10. Tolerance 6, Predictability 7, Control 8. 6 times 7 times 8 equals 336, divided by Surprise 3 equals 112. Higher scores mean variable charges feel manageable and even fair. Lower scores mean customers feel ambushed by their own bill, which drives complaints and cancellations.
content.closing_statement: Predictability turns variable costs into something users tolerate.

caption:
Principle Nº 68: Overage Psychology

Variable pricing must feel controlled, not punitive.

Ask yourself: If your business ever charges per-use or overage, how do you make it feel like the user is in control?
